text: The patient is positioned supine with arms tucked at the sides and legs straight, supported in approximately 20 degrees Trendelenburg. The surgical team uses a four-port configuration with one assistant port. The camera, equipped with a 30-degree down lens, is positioned in the right lower quadrant and will be adjusted to a 30-degree up lens. The ports are aligned approximately seven centimeters apart, located about four centimeters below the umbilicus, projecting upwards towards the patient's head. Instruments include hot shears in arm number three and a ProGrasp in arm number four.

The surgeon begins by creating an incision starting at the cecum and extending to the ligament of Treitz, cutting through the posterior peritoneum. The cut edges of the posterior peritoneum are sutured to the abdominal wall to maintain exposure and prevent the small bowel from obstructing the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the right iliac artery, ureter, and appendix, then makes an incision underneath the cecum through the posterior peritoneum. A flap is created and suspended to enhance exposure of the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the cut edge of the posterior peritoneum and begins creating a flap, working underneath the tissue to enhance exposure.

The surgeon uses bipolar forceps for blunt dissection, preferring them over the sharper Maryland dissector. A flap is being created with visible thickness, revealing bowel, bowel mesentery, and a lymph node within the mesentery.

The surgeon moves to the left side, revealing a cut edge and identifying postganglionic nerve fibers originating from the hypogastric plexus. The surgeon plans to preserve these nerve fibers if possible, depending on the surgical progress.

The surgeon identifies the left common iliac artery and discusses the lateral limit of the dissection, which is the ureter. The surgeon examines a structure that may be the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) while confirming the location of the common iliac artery.

The surgeon prepares to use two needle drivers and a long suspension suture to elevate the right cut edge. A 3-0 monofilament suture, likely a monocryl with an SH needle, is introduced for this purpose.

The surgeon adjusts the positioning to improve exposure, ensuring the small bowel remains stable without falling into the surgical field. A Hemolock is applied to secure structures, while the cecum is left intact to prevent the small bowel from shifting underneath.

The surgeon continues the dissection cephalad, preparing to take the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) for better exposure due to the left-sided tumor. The duodenum is visible, and a blue structure, likely the left renal vein or possibly the vena cava, is identified.
The surgeon identifies lymph node tissue adjacent to the renal vein, initially mistaking it for the inferior vena cava. The left renal vein is expected to be visible shortly.

The surgeon continues to define the left-sided flap, identifying the ureter and left cord. The inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) is visible on the side of the mesocolon, with the surgical team working underneath the left mesocolon in a distinct plane. Large lymphatic channels are observed, originating from the pancreas and extending over the left renal vein, which is currently visible.

The surgeon identifies a large lymphatic channel and prepares to clip it using a robotic clip applier. The lymphatic is visibly massive, and care is taken to prevent chylous ascites by securing the lymphatic channels with Hemolock clips.

The surgeon uses a robotic clip applier to secure large lymphatic channels over the left renal vein, ensuring they remain intact. The assistant adjusts the exposure to improve visibility, while the surgeon demonstrates the use of different instruments to achieve optimal angles during the procedure.
The surgeon uses scissors to navigate underneath the aorta, encountering additional lymphatic channels. The gonadal vein and a probable lumbar vein are visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon carefully dissects lymphatic tissue from a large artery, preparing to clip it. Hemolox clips are applied to secure the artery. The dissection begins from the lower part of the surgical field, progressing upwards, with the intention to take the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), ensuring good collateral flow to the colon.
The surgeon identifies the general femoral nerve and begins dissecting the cord, focusing on the left side of the aorta. The surgical field is prepared for further dissection.

The surgeon identifies a lymph node and clips it, while noting the cord crossing the ureter. The surgeon considers removing a small structure, possibly perinephric fat or an artery, using clips or a robotic vessel sealer.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to take the lumbar arteries and veins. The ureter crossing the iliac marks the lower boundary of the node dissection. Lymphatic tissue is being removed, with small branches deemed unimportant.

The surgeon pushes the iliac down, revealing the common iliac vein and a small branch coming off it. Clips are prepared for use at the distal extent of the para-aortic nodes, which are clearly visible. The surgeon requests a sharper pair of scissors for the procedure.

The surgeon identifies the sympathetic trunk, a white structure distinct from the general femoral nerve, and prepares to follow it upwards. The surgical team adjusts the instruments, pushing forward and then pulling back slightly to avoid iliac issues.
The surgeon identifies postganglionic fibers of the sympathetic trunk, noting their small size on the left side. A vessel is visible, and a minor bleeder is addressed in the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies nerve trunks, including the hypogastric plexus, located between the inferior vena cava and the aorta. The fibers are visible as they extend underneath the cava, and the surgeon continues exploration on the left side.
The surgeon introduces new scissors into the surgical field. The camera captures the initial exposure of the adrenal gland, with visible surrounding tissue and anatomical structures.
The surgeon clips the left-sided fibers, which are part of the sympathetic trunk, using sharp instruments.
The surgeon identifies the sympathetic chain and prepares to follow and take the gonadal vessels, indicating the next steps in the procedure.
The surgeon identifies a large postganglionic fiber coming off the sympathetic chain and prepares to cut it cold, with visible lymphatic structures nearby.

The surgeon identifies an open channel potentially causing a chylous leak and requests robotic clips to address the issue. A suction device is used to clean the area and monitor the effect of the intervention.

The surgeon uses clips to divide lymphatic channels near the internal cable to prevent spillage, separating the para-aortic, interaortic cable, and pre-aortic regions. The right and left renal arteries are visible during the procedure.

The surgeon uses a robotic clip to secure large lumbar lymphatics, followed by a vessel sealer to take the lumbar structure. The surgical field includes visible lymphatic channels and surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer in the left hand to address a minor issue after inadvertently affecting a lumbar structure. A suction device is used to clear the area for better visualization.

The surgeon identifies the anterior spinous ligament and proceeds to clip a cluster of lymphatics near the lumbar region, carefully avoiding a nearby vein. Suction is applied to clear the area, revealing an artery.

The surgeon exposes the cord and gonadal vein, ensuring clear visibility. The ureter is positioned far from the surgical field. A large lymphatic channel is identified, and suction is applied to clear the area.

The surgeon identifies the artery and uses suction to clear the field. The cord is being freed from surrounding tissue, with plans to address it later. Large lymphatic channels are visible, and the cruciate diaphragm is starting to be exposed.
The surgeon identifies a large vein and attempts to visualize underneath it, while an assistant holds back surrounding structures to maintain clear exposure.

The surgeon applies multiple clips to secure vessels and lymphatic channels, ensuring hemostasis and preventing lymphatic leakage. The surgical field is inspected for effective dissection and hemostasis before proceeding to the next area.
The surgeon uses a bipolar device to manage bleeding near the vena cava, preparing to separate the inter-caval and para-caval regions for potential nerve-sparing techniques.
The surgeon identifies the cord structure on one side and proceeds to work on the cable side, noting a small fiber. Suction is applied to clear the area.
The surgeon identifies a small lumbar fiber, asking an assistant to adjust their position for better visibility. The lumbar structure is noted to be very small.

The surgeon identifies three post-ganglionic trunks, which are small in size, and uses cold dissection to carefully trace and separate them from the surrounding lymphatics. The trunks are traced towards the hypogastric plexus, which is located on top of the lymphatic tissue.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the hypogastric plexus, focusing on preserving nerve trunks while removing lymphatic tissue. The left side trunk is cut, highlighting the challenge of sparing nerves on this side compared to the right. The procedure includes retro-aortic dissection to ensure complete removal of lymphatics.

The surgeon focuses on dissecting and preserving nerve trunks, working meticulously to avoid damage to small nerves. The suction device is used to clear the surgical field, revealing multiple nerve trunks, including a large trunk and smaller nerves below. The surgeon attempts to preserve the middle nerve trunk while directing the assistant to adjust traction.

The surgeon identifies and prepares to remove the lumbar structure, noting a clip from the opposite side. Attention is given to preserving a nearby bundle, potentially the cisterna chyli, while working on this side of the surgical field.
The surgeon uses scissors to expose a prominent lymphatic channel, which is then clipped to prevent leakage. The lymphatic structure is identified as significant, requiring careful handling.
The surgeon uses a grasper to manipulate the inter-aortic cable area, focusing on nerve sparing. The sigmoid colon is visible but not involved in the current procedure.

The surgeon identifies nerve fibers to be preserved and highlights a consistent vein prone to bleeding. The para-aortic lymph nodes on the right side are being addressed, with dissection extending to where the ureter crosses the right common iliac artery. The distal extent is clipped, and the lymph nodes are removed. The procedure will conclude with the removal of both spermatic cords from the inguinal canals.
The surgeon addresses a small bleeder under the cava, using suction to manage the bleeding. A large lymphatic vessel is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the adrenal vein and positions instruments near the renal pelvis and ureter. A clip is handed to the surgeon, who prepares to use scissors for further dissection.

The surgeon removes the right spermatic cord from the inguinal canal and proceeds to dissect the left cord. The completed lymph node dissections include paracaval, intertacable, retrocable, retroaortic, and peraortic nodes, with visible nerve-sparing bundles. The surgeon prepares to navigate around the sigmoid colon to access the left cord, noting some bleeding in the area.
The surgeon prepares to bag the specimens and applies FloSeal in the upper abdomen to create a clot, aiming to reduce lymphatic fluid leakage.

The surgeon carefully dissects the gonadal vessels as they cross anterior to the ureter, ensuring to avoid the ureter. The gonadal vessels are seen moving laterally while the ureter moves medially. Adjustments are made to the surgical instruments to improve access and visibility.
The camera view shifts to reveal the dissection area from the opposite side, focusing on the exposed cord structure.

The surgeon navigates around the left vas deferens, noting a vasal vessel prone to bleeding. The epigastric artery is visible, and the surgeon requests deeper exploration of the vessel. Sutures are observed, indicating a previous orchiectomy performed by another surgeon.
The surgeon requests an extraction sack to place the specimen into a small bag.
